title: On the single-Hessian Gaussian wavepacket dynamics
authors: Davide Barbiero, Jiří J. L. Vaníček
abstract: Single-Hessian Gaussian wavepacket dynamics (GWD) significantly reduces the computational burden of Heller's local harmonic GWD, while maintaining comparable accuracy in approximating vibronic spectra. Here, we provide a new, symplectic derivation of the equations of motion of single-Hessian GWD and show that, unlike the local harmonic version, this method conserves the non-canonical symplectic structure on the manifold of Gaussian wavepackets and$-$for bounded dynamics in smooth potentials$-$avoids the drift of energy. Our numerical results suggest that, despite being much more efficient than the local harmonic variant, the single-Hessian GWD exhibits the same $\mathcal{O}(\hbar)$ asymptotic error in averages of observables. To further accelerate numerical simulations, we implement high-order time-stepping geometric integrators that are time-reversible and conserve the norm and symplectic structure exactly, regardless of the time step. In addition, we present explicit expressions for the exact evolution of the width of a single-Hessian Gaussian wavepacket in a general potential, as well as for the exact evolution of the whole wavepacket in a global harmonic potential. Using on-the-fly ab initio Gaussian wavepacket dynamics on the first excited-state surface of ammonia, we numerically confirm the conservation of geometric properties by these integrators and demonstrate that high-order integrators can enhance both accuracy and computational efficiency. We also compute the photoelectron spectrum of the difluorocarbene anion and the absorption spectrum of methylamine, and find that, in comparison with experiment, single-Hessian GWD outperforms global harmonic models and matches the accuracy of local harmonic GWD. Finally, we identify which spectral features are sensitive to the choice of reference Hessian.
summary: This paper presents a more efficient version of Gaussian wavepacket dynamics (single-Hessian GWD) that maintains the accuracy of more expensive methods while being numerically more stable. By using symplectic integrators, the method avoids energy drift and preserves the underlying geometric structure of the wavepacket manifold. The approach is validated through successful simulations of complex molecular spectra.
main_problem: The computational cost of Heller's local harmonic Gaussian wavepacket dynamics (GWD) is high, and traditional methods often suffer from energy drift and lack of symplectic structure conservation.
main_result: The authors derive a symplectic formulation for single-Hessian GWD that conserves the non-canonical symplectic structure and avoids energy drift, achieving accuracy comparable to local harmonic GWD with significantly higher efficiency.
method: A new symplectic derivation of the equations of motion for single-Hessian GWD, implemented using high-order time-stepping geometric integrators that are time-reversible and norm-conserving.
model_or_system: The method is applied to molecular dynamics, specifically the first excited-state surface of ammonia, the photoelectron spectrum of the difluorocarbene anion, and the absorption spectrum of methylamine.
key_observables: Vibronic spectra, photoelectron spectrum, absorption spectrum, and the evolution of wavepacket width.
important_parameters: The asymptotic error scales as O(hbar); the choice of reference Hessian is identified as a key factor for spectral sensitivity.
main_assumptions: The dynamics are assumed to be bounded in smooth potentials to ensure energy conservation.
paper_structure: The paper provides a symplectic derivation of single-Hessian GWD, introduces high-order geometric integrators, presents analytical expressions for wavepacket evolution, and validates the method through ab initio simulations of ammonia, difluorocarbene anion, and methylamine.
why_interesting: It introduces a more efficient, numerically stable, and symplectic-preserving alternative to standard Gaussian wavepacket dynamics, which is crucial for large-scale molecular quantum dynamics simulations.
analysis_basis: abstract only; PDF text extraction failed or PDF unavailable
